risk_summary: Composite import-corridor risk has stepped up materially over the past 72 hours. Brent has climbed to 94.78 USD per barrel from 88.84 a week earlier, driven by an IRGC tanker boarding in the Strait of Hormuz on 28 June and a Houthi strike on a Liberia-flagged crude tanker in Bab el-Mandeb on 26 June. The Hormuz exposure is structurally severe given that ~40-45% of India's crude and 41.5% of LNG imports (predominantly Qatari) transit that chokepoint, while JKM LNG has surged to 17.84 USD per MMBtu on the same news flow. Coking coal is a parallel concern: Cyclone Marcia has disrupted Hay Point terminals, and prices have risen to 358.10 USD per tonne with Australia still supplying 67.4% of India coking coal demand. Recommend elevating crude SPR draw readiness at Vizag-Mangalore-Padur and accelerating Russian ESPO and US Gulf cargo lifting against Q3 nominations.
scenario_hormuz: Modeling a 14-day full Hormuz closure, India would lose access to approximately 2.4 mb/d of seaborne crude and 5.8 MTPA-equivalent of Qatari and UAE LNG. Strategic Petroleum Reserve of 39 MMb at Vizag, Mangalore, and Padur covers 9.5 days of imports at current run rates, leaving a 4-5 day gap that must be bridged by Russian Urals and ESPO via Cape of Good Hope (adds 14-18 days transit) and US WTI Midland already in the Atlantic basin. Refineries with the highest exposure are Vadinar (Nayara, 20.0 MMTPA, ~70% Middle East slate), Mangalore (MRPL, 15.0 MMTPA), and Kochi (BPCL, 15.5 MMTPA). LNG impact is more acute: Dahej terminal runs at 96.4% utilization against 17.5 MTPA capacity, leaving zero swing room. Recommend immediate diversion of two USGC cargoes already in Atlantic transit, activation of force majeure clauses on Qatari long-term contracts, and pre-positioning of two Suezmax tankers off Fujairah for Petroleum Reserves arbitrage.
scenario_australia_coal: Cyclone Marcia tracking over Queensland's Bowen Basin threatens 14-21 days of disruption at Hay Point, Dalrymple Bay, and Abbot Point terminals which collectively load ~75% of Australia coking coal exports. India imports 67.4% of coking coal from Australia, equivalent to roughly 38 MT annualized into Paradip, Vizag, and Dhamra. A three-week outage removes ~2.2 MT from the steel supply chain and lifts price exposure given current 358.10 USD per tonne benchmark. SAIL Bhilai and Tata Steel Jamshedpur carry 35-45 day inventories but JSW Vijayanagar runs leaner at 22 days. Recommend immediate spot procurement from US Appalachian (PCI grades), Mozambique Vale tenders, and Russian Elga via Vladivostok-Paradip; signal Ministry of Steel to relax import duty for emergency cargos through end-Q3.
scenario_china_rare_earth: MOFCOM has tightened export licenses on neodymium, dysprosium, and terbium effective 1 July, mirroring the 2023 gallium-germanium playbook. India imports 89.4% of rare earth needs from China and holds a modest 1200 tonne IREL stockpile concentrated in monazite-derived light rare earths. Neodymium oxide has rallied 18.8% in 30 days to 679,000 CNY per tonne and is likely to test the 720,000 level if license issuance slows. Acute exposure is in wind turbine permanent magnets (Suzlon, Inox Wind) and EV traction motors (Tata Motors, M&M); defense applications absorb less than 3% of volume but are politically sensitive. Recommend fast-tracking the IREL-Hindustan Copper rare earth separation JV, opening a strategic dialogue with Lynas Malaysia and Iluka Resources Australia for offtake, and front-loading 18 months of magnet inventory through Japanese trader Mitsui by end-August.
recommendation_crude: For July-August 2026 nominations, shift the marginal barrel away from Middle East sour and toward Russian Urals and US WTI Midland. Concretely: increase Russian volume from 36.2% to 42-44% of slate where Vadinar and Jamnagar configuration permits, secure two incremental US WTI cargoes per month for Mumbai and Kochi (BPCL), and reduce Iraqi Basrah Heavy by 3-4 cargoes. Maintain Saudi Arab Light at 13-14% as a baseload hedge and to preserve Aramco term relationship. Net realized landed cost should improve by 1.8-2.4 USD per barrel versus a Hormuz-weighted slate while reducing transit-risk premium by an estimated 0.6 USD per barrel based on current war-risk insurance quotes from Lloyds JWLA.
recommendation_lng: JKM at 17.84 USD per MMBtu makes spot procurement uneconomical against Petronet long-term Qatari pricing of approximately 12.40 USD per MMBtu landed Dahej. However, Hormuz contingency requires diversifying the lifting profile: increase US Sabine Pass and Corpus Christi DES cargoes for July-September by 4-6 spot tenders, accept reload economics from European storage where appropriate, and direct Kochi terminal (currently 28.4% utilization) and Ennore (42.6%) to absorb non-Qatari cargoes to balance pipeline grid loads. The Hazira-Dahej corridor should remain Qatar-dominated under HOA contracts. Initiate negotiation with QatarEnergy to invoke flexibility clauses on volume timing for August deliveries if Hormuz tensions escalate further.
executive_brief: India's energy import resilience is being tested simultaneously across three vectors this week. Crude and LNG face a Hormuz boarding incident plus continued Red Sea attacks pushing Brent to 94.78 and JKM to 17.84, with 41.5% of LNG and 40-45% of crude exposed to the chokepoint. Coking coal is hit by Cyclone Marcia closing Queensland terminals while prices touch 358.10 USD per tonne against 67.4% Australian dependence. Critical minerals see China tightening rare earth licenses with neodymium oxide up nearly 19% in 30 days against 89.4% Chinese import share. Recommended near-term actions: pre-authorize a 5 MMb SPR draw window, accelerate Russian and US Gulf crude liftings, divert two US LNG cargoes to Dahej-Hazira, activate Mozambique and US Appalachian coking coal contingencies, and front-load 18 months of NdPr magnet inventory through Japanese intermediaries. The combined commodity exposure justifies elevating the National Energy Security Index to Amber for at least 14 days.
chat_default: Ask me about a specific corridor (Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb, Malacca, South China Sea), a commodity in the basket, a sourcing scenario, the SPR plan, or any open feed item. I'll answer with the live composite scores, drivers, and the relevant recommended alternatives on screen right now.
impact_cascade_default:
Section 1 - What this hits first. The disruption strikes the commodities that physically depend on this cause. Where India's import dependence is high (crude 88%, LNG 50%, rare earths 90%, polysilicon 90%), even a partial cutoff transmits at near-full strength because there is no domestic buffer to absorb it. The directly hit commodities are the entry points; everything downstream follows from how deeply each one is woven into India's industrial base.

Section 2 - The chain into Indian sectors. Follow the highest-severity paths. A crude or LNG shock runs first into fuel retail and power, then LNG specifically feeds the urea plants - gas is the urea feedstock, so a gas shortfall becomes a fertilizer shortfall within roughly three weeks. Fertilizer scarcity then lands on agriculture ahead of the sowing window, and a weak crop feeds food inflation about two months out. A rare-earth or polysilicon shock instead runs into wind turbines, EV traction motors and the solar build-out, slowing the 500 GW clean-energy target and tightening grid-firming storage.

Section 3 - The hidden effect. The non-obvious consequence a procurement-only view misses is the fertilizer-to-food-inflation loop. A crude-and-LNG disruption reads on the dashboard as a fuel-price story, but the more damaging chain is LNG starving urea production, which compresses the kharif crop and pushes food - roughly 46% of the CPI basket - higher two months later. Food inflation, not pump prices, is what moves rural demand and the political calculus.

Section 4 - Macro and timing. Week one: commodity prices and INR move as the import bill widens. Weeks two-to-four: fuel and freight feed WPI and the direct CPI fuel weight. Month two-plus: food inflation lands through the agriculture chain, fiscal subsidy burden rises as the government cushions fertilizer and LPG, and the GDP drag shows up through dampened consumption and any industrial-power shortfall. The slow effects are the large ones.

Section 5 - What to do now. Match mitigations to the most damaging chains. For the crude/LNG chain: pre-authorise an SPR draw across Visakhapatnam-Mangalore-Padur, divert US Gulf and Atlantic-basin cargoes, and protect urea-plant gas allocation over merchant power to defend the fertilizer-food chain. For the China materials chain: front-load NdPr magnet and polysilicon inventory through Japanese and Korean intermediaries, accelerate the IREL rare-earth separation JV, and signal MNRE on solar-module duty timing. Run demand-side levers in parallel: retail price-passthrough smoothing for six weeks to dampen CPI, and a fertilizer subsidy buffer to hold the agriculture chain. Refinery, smelter and grade-compatibility validation remain outside this analysis and need domain-partner sign-off.
cascade_default:
Section 1 - Immediate impact (first 7 days). Strait of Hormuz at 100 cuts off 40-45% of India's crude and roughly 41% of LNG dispatch within the first 72 hours. Vadinar (Nayara, 20 MMTPA, ~70% Middle East slate) is the first to throttle, followed by Mangalore (MRPL, 15 MMTPA) and Kochi (BPCL, 15.5 MMTPA). Dahej LNG terminal at 96.4% utilisation has zero swing capacity; Hazira drops to keep grid pressure. Spot Brent jumps 18-25% inside 96 hours; JKM LNG widens 30-40% as Qatari long-term volumes get force-majeured.

Section 2 - Secondary cascade (days 7-30). Power sector fuel mix tilts back toward Indonesian coal; coking coal landed cost rises 8-12% as bulk carriers reposition. Petchem feedstock (naphtha, ATF) tightens; downstream LPG retail prices rise 6-9% before the subsidy buffer kicks in. Steel mills (Tata Bhilai, JSW Vijayanagar) keep running on 30-45 day stockpiles but next purchase cycle compresses margins by 200-350 bps. EV cell cost passthrough lags 21 days as cathode-makers buffer; solar project IRRs drop 80-120 bps if the disruption extends.

Section 3 - Macro effects (30+ days). Brent at 105-115/bbl baseline. INR/USD widens 80-150 bps as the import bill rises by 3-4 billion USD per month. GDP drag estimated at 35-50 bps annualised per month of disruption. WPI passthrough lands in CPI within 6 weeks (60-90 bps headline). SPR drawdown of 0.85 MMb/day from day 5 keeps the 9.5-day buffer falling to roughly 5.1 days at the 14-day mark.

Section 4 - Top 3 recommended alternatives. (1) Russia ESPO blend via Vladivostok-Paradip / Vadinar - lift 2 incremental cargoes (4 MMb) Aug-Sep; lead time 16-18 days via Cape if Suez also stressed; price discount of 3-5 USD/bbl to Brent absorbs much of the freight uplift; clears OFAC general license screening for Indian importers. Mitigates the immediate Vadinar throttle (Section 1) directly. (2) US WTI Midland via Sabine Pass to Vadinar / Jamnagar - already 4 cargoes in Atlantic transit; lead time 22-26 days; cost-neutral after the Atlantic basin re-routing; clears sanctions; mitigates the LNG cascade indirectly by freeing Qatari long-term volumes the US would normally compete with. (3) Iraqi Basrah Medium via Cape to Mangalore - keeps a Middle Eastern grade in the slate that Mangalore's hydrocracker is calibrated for; lead time 25-30 days via Cape adds insurance cost but avoids the cascade re-emerging if Hormuz partially reopens.

Section 5 - Guardrails and what's NOT solved. Russian volumes carry G7 price-cap compliance risk; cargoes purchased above the cap forfeit Western insurance. The three alternatives do NOT solve the LNG cascade - Dahej will remain throttled until US Sabine Pass spot cargoes are diverted (separate procurement decision). Demand-side levers running in parallel: fast-track 5 MMb SPR draw window across Vizag-Mangalore-Padur; tighten retail price passthrough for 6 weeks to dampen CPI; signal MoPNG to authorise import-duty relaxation for emergency cargoes. Refinery-grade compatibility for Russian and US grades into specific Indian configurations requires PESO and refinery-engineering validation outside this analysis.
